perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement shall be noted under 'Optional Extras and Considerations'.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the governing law clause to a section titled 'Optional Extras and Considerations' implies that the choice of law is not a mandatory element of the contract. This weakens the enforceability of the contract by suggesting the governing law is optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3 of the Rome I Regulation dictates that a contract shall be governed by the law chosen by the parties. The modification suggests the application of 'the laws of England and Wales' as an optional rather than definitive element of the agreement, contradicting the principle of freedom of choice and the binding nature of the selected governing law as outlined in Article 3. While the UK is no longer part of the EU, English courts would still consider Rome I as persuasive authority, and many contracts are drafted using its principles.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the laws of England and Wales govern the agreement. The modified contract places this governing law clause under 'Optional Extras and Considerations,' suggesting it is not a mandatory element. The Law Explanation cites Article 3 of the Rome I Regulation, which emphasizes the freedom of choice and the binding nature of the selected governing law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is from the EUR-Lex website and references Regulation (EC) No 593/2008 (Rome I), further supporting the importance of the governing law selection. By making the governing law optional, the changed text contradicts the binding nature of this selection, creating a legal contradiction and structural flaw as defined, because a required legal element is placed in a misleading section.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS whereof this Agreement has been duly executed as a deed the day and year first before written.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS whereof this Agreement has been noted as supplementary information the day and year first before written.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'IN WITNESS' clause to a section concerning supplementary information calls into question whether the agreement was ever intended to have legal force. The 'IN WITNESS' clause signifies the parties' intent to be legally bound, so changing it to supplementary information undermines that intent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Formation - Intent to Create Legal Relations
perturbation.law_citation: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
perturbation.law_explanation: The case of Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co establishes that for a contract to be formed, there must be an intention to create legal relations. By moving the 'IN WITNESS' clause, which signifies this intention, to supplementary information, the modification weakens the evidence of such intent, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable under English law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ERS v. MICHAEL A. NEWDOW et al. on writ of certiorari to the united states court of appeals for the ninth circuit [June 14, 2004] Justice Stevens delivered the opinion of the Court. Each day elementary school teachers in the Elk Grove Unified School District (School District) lead their classes in a group recitation of the Pledge of Allegiance. Respondent, Michael A. Newdow, is an atheist whose daughter participates in that daily exercise. Because the Pledge contains the words “under God,” he views the School District’s policy as a religious indoctrination of his child that violates the First Amendment . A divided panel of the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit agreed with Newdow. In light of the obvious importance of that decision, we granted certiorari to review the First Amendment issue and, preliminarily, the question whether Newdow has standing to invoke the jurisdiction of the federal courts. We conclude that Newdow lacks standing and therefore reverse the Court of Appeals’ decision. I “The very purpose of a national flag is to serve as a symbol of our country,” Texas v. Johnson, 491 U. S. 397 , 405 (1989), and of its proud traditions “of freedom, of equal opportunity, of religious tolerance, and of good will for other peoples who share our aspirations,” id ., at 437 (Stevens, J., dissenting). As its history illustrates, the Pledge of Allegiance evolved as a common public acknowledgement of the ideals that our flag symbolizes. Its recitation is a patriotic exercise designed to foster national unity and pride in those principles. The Pledge of Allegiance was initially conceived more than a century ago. As part of the nationwide interest in commemorating the 400th anniversary of Christopher Columbus’ discovery of America, a widely circulated national magazine for youth proposed in 1892 that pupils recite the following affirmation: “I pledge allegiance to my Flag and the Republic for which it stands: one Nation indivisible, with Liberty and Justice for all.” 1 In the 1920’s, the National Flag Conferences replaced the phrase “my Flag” with “the flag of the United States of America.” In 1942, in the midst of World War II, Congress adopted, and the President signed, a Joint Resolution codifying a detailed set of “rules and customs pertaining to the display and use of the flag of the United States of America.” Chapter 435, 56 Stat. 377 . Section 7 of this codification provided in full: “That the pledge of allegiance to the flag, ‘I pledge allegiance to the flag of the United States of America and to the Republic for which it stands, one Nation indivisible, with liberty and justice for all’, be rendered by standing with the right hand over the heart; extending the right hand, palm upward, toward the flag at the words ‘to the flag’ and holding this position until the end, when the hand drops to the side. However, civilians will always show full respect to the flag when the pledge is given by merely standing at attention, men removing the headdress. Persons in uniform shall render the military salute.” Id ., at 380. This resolution, which marked the first appearance of the Pledge of Allegiance in positive law, confirmed the importance of the flag as a symbol of our Nation’s indivisibility and commitment to the concept of liberty. Congress revisited the Pledge of Allegiance 12 years later when it amended the text to add the words “under God.” Act of June 14, 1954, ch. 297, 68 Stat. 249 . The House Report that accompanied the legislation observed that, “[f]rom the time of our earliest history our peoples and our institutions have reflected the traditional concept that our Nation was founded on a fundamental belief in God.” H. R. Rep. No. 1693, 83d Cong., 2d Sess., p. 2 (1954). The resulting text is the Pledge as we know it today: “I pledge allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America, and to the Republic for which it stands, one Nation under God, indivisible, with liberty and justice for all.” 4 U. S. C. §4 . II Under C
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw that undermines the contract's enforceability. The original text uses 'IN WITNESS whereof this Agreement has been duly executed as a deed,' which signifies the intent to be legally bound. The changed text replaces this with 'IN WITNESS whereof this Agreement has been noted as supplementary information,' calling into question whether the agreement was ever intended to have legal force. This contradiction falls under Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address contract law or the 'IN WITNESS' clause, but the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that the change weakens the evidence of intent to create legal relations, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 10.2 Any notice or other communication to be given under this Agreement must be in writing and must be delivered or sent by pre-paid first class post or email to the address of the relevant Party set out in this Agreement or to any other address that that Party may have notified to the other Party in writing in accordance with this clause.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 Any notice or other communication to be given under this Agreement might be placed into Section 15, further considerations, and may be delivered or sent by carrier pigeon.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the communication clause into the 'further considerations' section and including the option of 'carrier pigeon', the validity and legal certainty of how communications will be delivered is undermined, thus introducing doubt.
perturbation.law_citation: Household Fire & Carriage Accident Insurance Co Ltd v Grant (1879) 4 Ex D 216
perturbation.law_explanation: This case established the postal rule, under English contract law, that acceptance of a contract occurs when a letter is posted. By moving the notice clause to a less formal section of the agreement and specifying an unreliable means of communication, the modification casts doubt on how acceptance would occur. Therefore, it goes against the standard understanding of contract law and what constitutes a valid agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies reliable methods for delivering notices (post or email), ensuring clear communication. The changed text undermines this by suggesting communication might be placed in a vague 'further considerations' section and delivered by 'carrier pigeon,' an unreliable method. This introduces uncertainty about whether notices will be received, contradicting the need for clear and reliable communication in contractual agreements. While the scraped law snippet is irrelevant, the structural flaw of placing a critical clause (notice) in an ambiguous section and the introduction of an unreliable communication method directly contradict the established legal principles of valid contractual communication, as highlighted in the Law Explanation regarding the importance of clear acceptance terms. This falls under the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction because a critical part of the contract is placed in a section that undermines clarity, making it difficult to enforce. The carrier pigeon creates further doubt, severely weakening the legal certainty of communication.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall treat as confidential all Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any other person nor use Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Permitted Purpose.  The Receiving Party shall ensure that all Confidential Information is protected with security measures and a degree of care that would be applied by a reasonable business person to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall treat as confidential all Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any other person nor use Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Permitted Purpose, this may also be referred to in 'Section 18, Party Amenities'.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the confidentiality of the information may also be detailed in a section called 'Party Amenities,' it diminishes the seriousness and importance of the confidentiality clause, suggesting it may be treated as an optional perk instead of a vital obligation.
perturbation.law_citation: Coco v A.N. Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: The case of Coco v A.N. Clark (Engineers) Ltd establishes the equitable doctrine of confidence. The three elements for breach of confidence are: the information must have the necessary quality of confidence, it must have been imparted in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence, and there must have been an unauthorised use of that information. By moving it to a section called 'Party Amenities,' it suggests that the confidentiality itself is of minor significance. This weakens the argument there was the necessary quality of confidence to make it a breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: intellectual property | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you intellectual property Intellectual property (I.P. or IP) is a type of property encompassing the products of original human thought. Common examples of intellectual property include: the contents of a book, designs of an invention, computer software, company logos, and music. Intellectual property differs from other forms of property in that it is non-tangible, and non-rivalrous. Unlike other forms of property, intellectual property can be used by infinitely many people without depriving the original owner of the use of their property. This characteristic of intellectual property makes it particularly vulnerable to free rider problems, creating unique challenges for authors and inventors who wish to monetize their work. Intellectual property rights were created to ensure that authors and inventors are compensated for their efforts to incentivize the production of further works for the benefit of the public. They combat the free rider problem by giving intellectual property owners a near monopoly over the use of their creations for a set period of time. This allows intellectual property owners to license their intellectual property, and to recover monetarily from those who infringe on their intellectual property rights through piracy or wrongful use. However, intellectual property rights are not absolute, and are subject to a myriad of exceptions and affirmative defenses , such as the fair use doctrine. Within the broad category of intellectual property, there are four subcategories of intellectual property: patent , copyright , trademark , and trade secrets . In the United States, patents and copyright are regulated exclusively by federal law, as outlined in the Intellectual Property Clause . Trademarks are areas of shared jurisdiction between federal and state governments, with the federal government deriving their power to regulate trademarks through the Commerce Clause . Trade secrets are largely regulated by states through unfair competition laws. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords property PROPERTY RIGHTS intellectual property U.S. CONSTITUTION constitutional law Wex CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities PROPERTY business law wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions constitutional law intellectual property copyright law patent law trade secrets law trademark law property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states the Receiving Party's obligation to treat Confidential Information with security measures. The changed text diminishes the importance of this obligation by suggesting it may be found in 'Section 18, Party Amenities'. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that placing confidentiality in a section called 'Party Amenities' suggests that the confidentiality itself is of minor significance, weakening the argument that there was a necessary quality of confidence. This creates a structural flaw where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, which aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction'. Although there are no directly applicable scraped law snippets, the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the altered text all point to a contradiction. The original text treated confidentially as a strict legal requirement, the changed text weakens this.
